title: Anger At History Books That Erased Your People
dek: *When you open a history book and find your ancestors missing, your nervous system registers theft. The rage that follows isn't irrational. It's your body recognizing that someone decided your people's story wasn't worth telling, and that decision still shapes the world you navigate today. This anger deserves your attention, not your shame.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Anger at erased history is a somatic response to collective silencing, not a character flaw. Your body remembers what textbooks omitted, and that fury holds vital information about identity and belonging. Learning to work with this anger instead of suppressing it restores agency and opens pathways to healing.

You've been told that anger about the past is unproductive. That lie keeps you small. It keeps you quiet. And it keeps the very structures that erased your people humming along undisturbed. The fury you feel when you discover what was left out, what was whitewashed, what was rewritten to make colonizers look noble is not a problem to be managed. It's information. Your body is telling you something true about power, about whose stories get canonized, and about the price you've paid for that selective storytelling. In our experience working with clients who've discovered their erasure in mainstream narratives, this anger often arrives with a particular flavor. It's not the hot flash of road rage or the sharp spike of betrayal. It's something older, something that vibrates in your bones. Because this isn't just about facts on a page. It's about identity, belonging, and the right to claim your lineage without having to excavate it from footnotes. ## The Body Keeps Score When the Books Don't Peter Levine's work on trauma reminds us that the body holds what the mind cannot fully process. When you read a sanitized version of history that omits genocide, slavery, forced assimilation, or cultural destruction, your nervous system may respond as if to a present threat. Because in many ways, it is. The erasure continues. The same systems that decided your people weren't worth a chapter are still operating. They're in hiring practices, in whose neighborhoods get investment, in which languages are considered "professional," in whose pain gets taken seriously at the doctor's office. Your anger isn't about dusty textbooks. It's about the living consequences of those editorial choices. You might notice this anger as tension in your jaw. As a clenched feeling in your solar plexus. As heat rising up the back of your neck when someone casually repeats a sanitized version of an event you know devastated your community. That's not dysfunction. That's your body refusing to pretend that what happened didn't matter. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the wisdom of somatic responses. Your body is not betraying you when it reacts to historical erasure. It's protecting something precious: the truth of your people's experience. ## When "Civilization" Means Your Grandmother's Language Was Beaten Out of Her The specific shape of this anger often depends on what was taken. For some, it's language. You might feel a particular grief-rage when you realize you can't speak to your elders in their mother tongue because residential schools, assimilation programs, or colonial education policies made that transmission impossible. For others, it's land. You know your people lived somewhere for thousands of years, and now that place is a parking lot, a golf course, a "discovery site" where your ancestors are described as if they were geological features rather than human beings with cosmologies and governance systems. For many, it's the flattening of complexity. Your people had trade routes, astronomical knowledge, agricultural innovations, literary traditions, and they're reduced to a single paragraph about "primitive tribes" or "simple farmers" who were fortunate enough to be "discovered" and "civilized." That last word, civilized, often triggers a particular quality of rage. Because you know what it meant. It meant forced conversion. It meant children stolen. It meant ceremonial objects locked in museums. It meant being told that everything your people knew about healing, about governance, about relationship with land was worthless compared to the systems being imposed. When you hold a history textbook that uses the word "civilization" to describe what was done to your people, your hands might shake. That's not excessive emotion. That's your body saying: *This is not okay. This has never been okay.* ## The Anger That Comes From Discovering What You Weren't Taught There's a particular moment many people describe: the first time they read an honest account of what happened to their people. Not the version from their high school textbook, but the version from their grandmother, from community historians, from scholars who didn't have to sanitize the truth to get published in mainstream presses. That moment often brings a wave of rage so intense it's almost disorienting. Because suddenly you realize how much you weren't told. How many years you spent internalizing narratives that made your people's suffering sound inevitable, natural, or even beneficial. Soraya Chemaly's research on women's anger has shown how certain groups are systematically taught that their anger is inappropriate, ugly, or dangerous. This applies equally to communities whose histories have been erased. You've been taught that dwelling on the past is divisive. That bringing up old wounds prevents healing. That anger about historical injustice makes you bitter, makes you a victim, makes you someone who can't move forward. But here's what they don't tell you: not knowing your history is its own kind of violence. And the anger that comes when you finally learn the truth is a sign that something in you refused to accept the lie. ## Your Nervous System Knows the Difference Between History and Propaganda Pat Ogden's work on sensorimotor psychotherapy helps us understand how the body processes and stores overwhelming information. When you encounter a historical account that contradicts what you were taught, your nervous system has to reconcile two competing narratives. One narrative says: "This is the story of progress, of development, of how we got to where we are today." The other says: "This is the story of theft, of genocide, of systematic destruction that created wealth for some and devastation for others." Your body often knows which one is true before your mind catches up. You might feel nauseous reading the sanitized version. You might feel a strange relief, despite the horror, when you read the honest account. Because finally, something matches what your body has been trying to tell you all along. This recognition can bring up anger at your teachers, at the curriculum committees, at the publishers who made editorial choices that shaped what you learned about yourself and your people. In my own practice, I've seen how this anger can be particularly sharp when directed at educational institutions that claimed to be objective, neutral, fact-based. Because you trusted them. And they lied to you. Some people find that keeping a journal specifically for these discoveries helps metabolize the anger. When you read something that contradicts what you were taught, write both versions. Notice what your body does with each. Notice where you feel the truth of your people's experience, even when it's painful. ## The Rage of Seeing Your Oppressors Called Heroes Open a U.S. history textbook and you'll find George Washington described as the father of his country. You might not find that he enslaved over 300 people. Open a Canadian history textbook and you'll read about John A. Macdonald's role in confederation. You might not read his own words about using starvation as a tool to clear Indigenous people from land needed for the railroad. When you discover that the people you were taught to admire built their legacies on your people's suffering, the anger can feel volcanic. Because it's not just that they did terrible things. It's that those terrible things were repackaged as heroism, as vision, as the difficult but necessary choices of great men. Your anger at this particular form of erasure is warranted. It's not "rewriting history" to tell the truth. It's not "judging the past by today's standards" to acknowledge that people at the time knew genocide was wrong, knew slavery was wrong, knew cultural destruction was wrong, because your people were telling them so, loudly, and being killed for it. You might find yourself unable to walk past certain statues without feeling that anger rise. You might struggle to stay quiet when someone casually praises a historical figure who signed the order that displaced your great-grandparents. That's not you being oversensitive. That's you refusing to participate in the lie. If you're looking for ways to work with the physical intensity of this anger, you might explore the resources on [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) for nervines that support the nervous system during periods of activation. [doTERRA Frankincense Essential Oil (15 mL)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B003I6Q3IG?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be grounding when you're working through difficult historical material and need to stay present with your body's responses. ## When Your Children Ask Questions You Weren't Allowed to Ask Many people report that this anger intensifies when they become parents or educators themselves. Because now you're responsible for passing on knowledge to the next generation, and you have to decide: do you teach them the sanitized version you were taught, or do you tell them the truth and watch them carry the same anger you carry? This is not an abstract dilemma. It plays out in homework assignments about Thanksgiving, in school projects about "explorers," in field trips to historical sites where the plaques tell only part of the story. You watch your child absorb the same lies you absorbed, or you intervene and make them the kid who corrects the teacher, who knows too much, who makes adults uncomfortable. The anger at being put in this position is legitimate. You shouldn't have to choose between protecting your child from painful truths and protecting them from harmful lies. But that's the inheritance of erasure. It doesn't end with your generation. It keeps demanding something from you. Some parents find it helpful to create alternative learning resources at home. Not to replace formal education entirely, but to provide context, counternarrative, and truth-telling that the curriculum won't offer. This can be exhausting work, and it's okay to feel angry that it's necessary. Over the years I've seen families develop their own rituals around historical honesty. One family marks the dates of events that textbooks omit with special meals, storytelling, and acknowledgment. Another keeps a "truth library" of books by historians from their community. These practices don't eliminate the anger, but they channel it toward preservation and transmission rather than letting it turn inward as shame or despair. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Working With Historical Anger When you feel that specific rage rising as you encounter erasure or distortion, try this: Stand with your feet hip-width apart. Press your feet into the ground and notice the contact. Say out loud or silently: "My people existed. My people exist. My people will exist." Let your body respond however it wants to those words. Some people's hands form fists. Some people's spines straighten. Some people cry. Don't control the response. Now place one hand on your heart and one on your belly. Breathe into both hands for five breaths. On each exhale, imagine your anger flowing down through your feet into the earth. Not to get rid of it, but to let it connect with something larger than your individual body. Your anger is part of an old conversation. It has roots. When you're ready, ask yourself: "What does this anger want me to do?" Not what you think you should do, but what the anger itself is pointing toward. Sometimes it's research. Sometimes it's conversation. Sometimes it's rest, because you've been carrying this for others and you need to set it down for a bit. Write down whatever comes. Don't judge it. This is information from your body about what you need in order to stay connected to truth without being consumed by rage. ## The Anger That Leads to Reclamation Here's what they don't tell you about anger at historical erasure: it can be generative. Not always, and not automatically, but when you work with it instead of suppressing it, this anger often becomes the fuel for reclaiming what was taken. People start language revitalization programs because they're angry their grandparents were punished for speaking their mother tongue. People create alternative curricula because they're angry their children are being taught lies. People write books, make films, build archives, and establish cultural centers because they're angry that their people's stories have been buried. This doesn't make the anger comfortable. It doesn't make it easy to live with. But it does mean the anger has somewhere to go besides your body, your relationships, your sense of hope about the future. You might explore some of the assessments available on [the assessments hub](/assessments) to better understand your own anger patterns and how they intersect with historical trauma. Recognizing your particular flavor of activation can help you channel the energy more skillfully. For some people, the reclamation work is public and visible. For others, it's quiet and personal. You might learn a traditional craft your great-grandmother practiced. You might cook foods that were part of your people's foodways before colonization. You might visit places that matter to your community's history and simply bear witness. All of this is reclamation. All of it says: what you tried to erase is still here. If you find that the physical intensity of this work leaves you depleted, [Natural Vitality Calm Magnesium Powder, Raspberry Lemon, 16oz](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00BPUY3W0?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support your nervous system's recovery during periods of sustained activation. ## When the Anger Becomes Ancestral Grief Sometimes, as you work with this anger over time, it transforms. The sharp heat becomes something deeper, more oceanic. You realize you're not just angry for yourself. You're angry for your great-great-grandmother who died without seeing justice. You're angry for the children who never got to grow up knowing their people's stories. You're angry for all the futures that were stolen. This is when anger becomes grief, and grief becomes a kind of love. Because you can't grieve what you don't love. The fact that you feel this depth of anger about what was taken from your people means you recognize its value. You know what was lost. And that knowing, painful as it is, is also a form of preservation. You're carrying something forward. The anger is part of how you carry it. Not the only part, and not the part you want to pass down unchanged to the next generation, but a part that matters. A part that says: this was not okay, this will never be okay, and I will not pretend otherwise to make anyone comfortable. Some people find that working with adaptogens helps them sustain this long-term engagement with difficult historical material. [Organic India Brahmi Gotu Kola](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0041V8F3S?tag=spankyspinola-20) has traditionally been used to support mental clarity and resilience during periods of intense learning and processing. You might also appreciate the perspectives shared on [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) about working with emotion as spiritual practice. There's wisdom about emotional regulation and mental health that can support this work, including guidance from the NIMH page on emotional regulation, though it's worth noting that mainstream mental health frameworks don't always account for the specific challenges of processing collective historical trauma. ## What Your Anger Knows That Forgiveness Culture Doesn't You've probably heard that you need to forgive. That holding onto anger about the past will poison you. That the only way to heal is to let go. But what if your anger is already doing healing work? What if it's the thing that keeps you connected to truth when everything around you is pushing you toward convenient amnesia? Forgiveness culture often serves the interests of people who benefit from historical erasure. It's easy to call for forgiveness when your people weren't the ones erased. It's easy to advocate for "moving on" when you're not the one who has to excavate your history from archives that were designed to obscure it. Your anger might be doing something forgiveness can't do. It might be maintaining a necessary boundary. It might be saying: I will not participate in pretending this didn't happen. I will not sacrifice truth for the comfort of people who don't want to acknowledge how they benefit from my people's dispossession. This doesn't mean living in a constant state of rage. It doesn't mean you can't also feel joy, connection, hope, and peace. But it does mean you get to decide what to do with your anger, not the people whose comfort depends on your silence. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### How do I deal with anger at family members who've internalized the sanitized version of history? This is particularly painful because it means the erasure has worked its way into your own family's understanding of who you are. Start by recognizing that internalized narratives are a survival strategy. Your family members may have needed to believe the sanitized version in order to function in a world that punished them for claiming the truth. Approach these conversations with curiosity rather than judgment when possible. Share what you're learning without demanding they accept it immediately. Sometimes planting seeds is enough. Sometimes you have to accept that different generations carry this differently, and your job is to break the cycle for yourself and those who come after you, not to convert everyone who came before. ### Is it okay to feel angrier about historical erasure than about personal slights? Yes. Completely. The scale of historical erasure is massive, and it affects every aspect of your life in ways that personal conflicts don't. Your anger is proportional to the harm. Don't let anyone tell you that you're "too focused on the past" or that you should care more about present-day interpersonal dynamics. Historical erasure *is* a present-day issue. It shapes your access to resources, your sense of identity, your children's education, and your community's wellbeing right now. The anger you feel is appropriate to the scope of what was taken. ### What if learning the truth about what happened to my people makes me too angry to function? First, if you're experiencing symptoms that interfere with daily life, work with a therapist who understands historical trauma, particularly someone from your community if possible. That said, what often feels like "too much anger to function" is actually your nervous system trying to process information it was never given space to process before. You might need to dose your exposure to difficult historical material. Read for thirty minutes, then do something grounding. Research one event, then take a break for a week. This isn't avoidance; it's pacing. You're learning to metabolize truth that's been kept from you your whole life. That takes time. Be patient with your system as it integrates what it's learning. You can find some grounding practices on [the about page](/about) that might help you stay resourced during this process. ## The Anger That Refuses to Disappear Your anger at erased history isn't going anywhere, and maybe that's exactly as it should be. Maybe that anger is doing important work in the world. Maybe it's keeping alive a refusal that needs to stay alive. You might wake up some days and feel tired of carrying it. You might wish you could just accept the sanitized version and move through the world without this constant friction between what you know and what you're told. But you can't unknow what you know. And your body won't let you pretend that erasure is acceptable just because it's common. This anger connects you to everyone else who's ever opened a history book and found their people missing. It connects you to the ones who came before you and insisted on telling the truth even when it cost them everything. It connects you to the ones who'll come after you and need to know that someone refused to let the lies stand unchallenged. You're not carrying this anger alone, even when it feels lonely. You're part of a long tradition of people who looked at what was written about them and said: no. That's not what happened. Let me tell you what actually happened. And then they did the work of preservation, reclamation, and truth-telling that makes it possible for you to know your own story now. Your anger is part of that work. It's not the enemy of your healing. It might be one of the tools. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on anger at history books that erased your people, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
